title: Macular Degeneration (AMD)
oneLiner: Progressive degeneration of the macula causing irreversible central vision loss; leading cause of blindness in over-50s; requires early detection to preserve reading and driving ability.

sections.Age-Related Macular Degeneration (AMD):
**One-liner:** Progressive degeneration of the macula causing irreversible central vision loss; leading cause of blindness in over-50s; requires early detection to preserve reading and driving ability.

---
sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
A 78-year-old woman presents with a two-month history of “a blurry patch in the middle of my vision” when reading. She also notices that straight door frames appear wavy. She has a 40-pack-year smoking history and her mother went blind in old age. On examination, her visual acuity is 6/18 in the right eye and 6/9 in the left. Fundoscopy reveals numerous yellow-white spots at the macula in both eyes, with a grey-green elevation and a small subretinal hemorrhage in the right eye.

**Dissecting the clues:**
- **Age >65 and gradual onset** → onset is typically insidious for dry AMD; sudden onset of distortion suggests conversion to wet AMD.
- **“Blurry patch in centre, not sides”** → macula disease spares peripheral fields; patients complain of central scotoma.
- **Metamorphopsia (wavy lines)** → highly specific for choroidal neovascularisation (CNV) — fluid under the neurosensory retina distorts photoreceptors.
- **Smoking history** → single strongest modifiable risk factor; oxidative stress and complement activation.
- **Family history** → genetic variants in CFH and ARMS2 are strong predictors; ask about blindness in parents/siblings.
- **Yellow-white spots (drusen)** → hallmark of early AMD; they are deposits of lipids and protein between the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) and Bruch’s membrane.
- **Grey-green elevation and hemorrhage** → classic signs of CNV; the “green” colour is the pigment epithelial detachment; blood indicates active leak.

**Atypical presentations:**
1. **Asymptomatic drusen** → many patients have drusen found incidentally during a routine eye exam; they may never progress. The trap is assuming they are benign — 15% develop advanced AMD within 5 years.
2. **Rapid vision loss in one eye (over hours to days)** → can mimic a retinal artery occlusion or inflammatory papillitis. Always check for a relative afferent pupillary defect (RAPD) and perform OCT to look for subretinal fluid.
3. **Bilateral symmetric dry AMD with no drusen** → geographic atrophy (dry advanced AMD) appears as sharply demarcated zones of RPE atrophy; the patient may not notice until fovea is involved. Can be mistaken for retinal dystrophies if the classic drusen are absent.

---
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
Age-related macular degeneration is a chronic, progressive degeneration of the macula — the central 5 mm of the retina responsible for high-acuity vision (reading, face recognition, driving). It occurs in two forms: **non-exudative (dry)** , characterised by drusen and geographic atrophy, and **exudative (wet)** , driven by choroidal neovascularisation that leaks fluid and blood under the retina.

**Why it matters:**
- **Frequency:** AMD affects 1 in 7 people over 50 globally; it is the leading cause of irreversible central vision loss in people over 65 in the developed world.
- **Stakes:** Patients lose the ability to read, recognise faces, and safely navigate — the psychological and functional impact rivals that of hip fracture or stroke.
- **Common pitfalls:** Junior doctors often dismiss “floaters and flashes” as benign vitreous detachment without checking central vision or performing Amsler grid testing. They also mistake the distortion of wet AMD for migraine aura or dry eye. Early diagnosis of wet AMD is a true emergency — every day of delay reduces the chance of functional recovery.

---
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind th:
The story of AMD begins with the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE), a single layer of cells that nourishes photoreceptors, recycles visual pigments, and pumps fluid out of the subretinal space. With age, oxidative stress from high metabolic demand and light exposure damages RPE cells. In genetically susceptible individuals (CFH gene variant reduces complement factor H activity), the RPE cannot clear debris effectively. This debris accumulates as **drusen** — yellow-white excrescences beneath the RPE.

**Why drusen appear:** The RPE sheds lysosomal material into Bruch’s membrane; when clearance fails, lipid and protein aggregates form drusen. On fundoscopy they look like yellow “spots”; they are the earliest clinical sign.

**Why vision blurs in dry AMD:** As drusen enlarge and confluent drusen develop, the RPE becomes dysfunctional. Photoreceptors begin to die, especially in the fovea. Eventually, the RPE atrophies in large confluent patches — **geographic atrophy**. The photoreceptors over these patches are gone, producing a well-demarcated scotoma. The patient notices blurring but often compensates with the other eye until the fovea is affected.

**Why vision distorts in wet AMD:** Chronic inflammation and RPE stress upregulate vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF). VEGF diffuses from the RPE to the choriocapillaris, promoting growth of abnormal new blood vessels through Bruch’s membrane into the sub-RPE or subretinal space. These **choroidal neovascular membranes (CNV)** are fragile and leak fluid (serous detachment) and blood (subretinal hemorrhage). The fluid separates photoreceptors from the RPE, causing them to become edematous and misaligned — this produces **metamorphopsia** (wavy lines). The grey-green elevation seen on fundoscopy is the pigment epithelial detachment caused by fluid accumulating under the RPE.

**Connecting to the pattern recognition cues:**
- Drusen → early AMD on exam.
- Metamorphopsia + sudden onset → active CNV → needs urgent anti-VEGF.
- Subretinal hemorrhage on fundoscopy → confirms wet AMD.
- Symmetric slow central vision loss over months to years → dry AMD with geographic atrophy.

---
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (Organised in Bins):
| Category | Cause / Trigger | Stem clue that points to it |
|----------|----------------|----------------------------|
| **Idiopathic** | Age >50 | “No other cause found in an elderly patient” |
| **Genetic** | CFH Y402H polymorphism, ARMS2 A69S variant | “Family history of blindness from AMD in a first-degree relative” |
| **Environmental** | Cigarette smoking (10+ pack-years) | “Patient smokes 20 per day” — strongest modifiable risk |
| **Dietary** | Low dietary antioxidants (zinc, lutein, vitamins C/E) | “Poor diet, BMI >30” |
| **Vascular** | Hypertension, cardiovascular disease | “Hypertensive, on statin” |
| **Light exposure** | Chronic sunlight (blue light) | “Worked outdoors for decades without sunglasses” |
| **Drug-related** | Hydroxychloroquine (rare cause of bull’s-eye maculopathy — mimic) | “Long-term hydroxychloroquine for RA” — different pathology |

**Most common cause:** Age alone is the dominant factor. Almost all cases are idiopathic, with genetic predisposition and smoking as the strongest accelerators.

---
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria:** The **AREDS classification** (Age-Related Eye Disease Study) grades AMD into categories:
- Category 1: No drusen or few small drusen (<63 µm) — essentially normal aging.
- Category 2: Multiple small drusen or a few medium drusen (63–124 µm) — early AMD.
- Category 3: Many medium drusen or at least one large drusen (≥125 µm) OR geographic atrophy not involving fovea — intermediate AMD.
- Category 4: Geographic atrophy involving fovea OR CNV — advanced AMD.

**Key investigations:**

| Test | What it shows | Why you order it | How to interpret |
|------|---------------|------------------|------------------|
| **Fundoscopy** | Drusen, RPE changes, subretinal fluid/hemorrhage | First-line; office-based | Presence of drusen = AMD; hemorrhage or grey-green elevation = wet AMD |
| **Optical coherence tomography (OCT)** | Cross-sectional retinal layers; shows drusen, intraretinal fluid, subretinal fluid, PED | Gold standard for wet AMD detection and monitoring | Fluid under the retina (subretinal) or within retina (intraretinal) = active CNV; hyperreflective drusen = dry AMD |
| **Fluorescein angiography (FA)** | Leakage of dye from CNV in wet AMD | Confirms active neovascularisation and classifies type of CNV (type 1 sub-RPE, type 2 subretinal) | Well-defined “lacy” hyperfluorescence with late leakage = classic CNV; ill-defined = occult CNV |
| **Amsler grid** | Patient self-monitoring for distortion or scotoma | Home screening for conversion to wet AMD | New distortion or missing squares = possible CNV → urgent review |
| **Autofluorescence** | Geographic atrophy margins | Quantifies progression of dry AMD | Dark areas with hyperautofluorescent borders = active atrophy expansion |

**Single finding that confirms wet AMD:** Choroidal neovascularisation seen on OCT (subretinal hyperreflective material with fluid) or FA (leaking CNV in the late phase).

**Single finding that rules out wet AMD:** Dry OCT without any fluid, combined with no leakage on FA (if performed).

---
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour) — Suspected wet AMD:**
- **Do not** start any drops or steroids. Do **not** apply pressure to the eye.
- **Immediate referral to ophthalmology** (same-day urgent): Wet AMD is a medical emergency. Vision can be lost permanently within hours to days.
- **No investigations on the ward** beyond checking visual acuity (Snellen chart), pupil reactivity, and performing a basic fundoscopy (if trained). The only definitive test is OCT/FA in the eye department.
- **Why:** Anti-VEGF therapy is most effective when given within 1–2 weeks of symptom onset. Delay reduces final visual acuity gains.

**Ward (24–72 hours) — After ophthalmology assessment:**
- **Anti-VEGF injection** (intravitreal): Ranibizumab 0.5 mg in 0.05 mL, or aflibercept 2 mg in 0.05 mL, or bevacizumab (off-label) 1.25 mg in 0.05 mL. Administered in a dedicated eye treatment room under topical anaesthesia.
- **Post-injection monitoring:** Check intraocular pressure if the patient has pain (transient rise is common; can give topical beta-blocker). Instruct patient regarding warning signs of endophthalmitis (pain, redness, decreased vision) — return if these occur within 1 week.
- **Educate:** Show the patient how to use an Amsler grid daily. Explain that distortion means recurrence — do not wait for an appointment.
- **If dry AMD (no fluid):** No urgent treatment. Start AREDS2 supplements (see below) if intermediate or advanced dry AMD in at least one eye.

**Long-term — Dry AMD:**
- **AREDS2 formula (age-related eye disease study 2):** Vitamin C 500 mg, vitamin E 400 IU, zinc oxide 80 mg, copper 2 mg (as cupric oxide), lutein 10 mg, zeaxanthin 2 mg. One capsule twice daily.
- **Why:** The AREDS/AREDS2 trials showed a 25–30% reduction in progression to advanced AMD in patients with intermediate or advanced dry AMD. It does **not** reverse vision loss.
- **Who gets it:** Anyone with Category 3 or 4 AMD in either eye (i.e., large drusen or geographic atrophy in at least one eye, or wet AMD in the fellow eye).
- **Lifestyle modifications:** Smoking cessation, control of hypertension, weight loss, blue-light protective sunglasses.
- **Low vision aids:** For patients with significant central scotoma, offer magnifiers, telescopes, and electronic reading devices. Refer to low vision clinic.

**What NOT to do:**
- **Do not give AREDS2 supplements to patients with only early AMD (small drusen only)** — no proven benefit and may cause harm (excess zinc can cause copper deficiency anaemia; high-dose vitamin E linked to increased prostate cancer risk).
- **Do not treat dry AMD with anti-VEGF** — no benefit and risks infection/retinal detachment.
- **Do not prescribe oral carbonic anhydrase inhibitors for central serous chorioretinopathy if you suspect wet AMD** — that is a different disease.
- **Do not tell a patient with wet AMD “it’s just cataracts”** — cortical cataracts cause glare, not metamorphopsia.

---
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | AMD (wet) | Central Serous Chorioretinopathy (CSC) | Diabetic Macular Edema (DME) | Epiretinal Membrane (ERM) |
|---------|-----------|----------------------------------------|------------------------------|---------------------------|
| **Pain** | None | None | None | None |
| **Laterality** | Usually unilateral (wet) or bilateral (dry) | Typically unilateral | Often bilateral | Usually unilateral |
| **Age** | >65 | <50, male predominance | Any age with diabetes | >50 |
| **Onset** | Subacute (days to weeks) for wet; gradual for dry | Acute (days) | Chronic, fluctuates | Very gradual (months to years) |
| **Metamorphopsia** | Yes (fluid distorts retina) | Yes (serous detachment distorts) | Yes (cystoid edema distorts) | Yes (membrane contracts retina) |
| **Fundoscopy** | Drusen + subretinal fluid/hemorrhage | Well-circumscribed serous detachment (clear fluid, no drusen) | Hard exudates, microaneurysms, dot-blot hemorrhages, retinal thickening | Cellophane-like shimmer on macular surface, vessels tortuous |
| **OCT** | Subretinal fluid, PED, CNV | Serous PED or subretinal fluid without CNV; absent drusen | Intraretinal cysts, increased central thickness, exudates | Epiretinal hyperreflective membrane, macular pseudohole |
| **FA** | Leaking CNV (classic or occult) | Ink-blot leak or smokestack from RPE | Microaneurysms + diffuse leakage | Staining of membrane, no leak |
| **Key differentiator** | Drusen + CNV on OCT | No drusen, young, stressed, unilateral serous detachment | History of diabetes + hard exudates | Pearly membrane on OCT without fluid |

---
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
**1. Irreversible central vision loss (wet AMD)**
- **Presentation:** Sudden drop in visual acuity to counting fingers or hand motion. The CNV bleeds or forms a fibrovascular scar (disciform scar). The scar is white/yellow on fundoscopy — it replaces the macula permanently.
- **Key signs:** No further distortion (because the retina is fixed by fibrosis), dense central scotoma, RAPD if very severe.
- **What to do:** None can reverse a disciform scar. Prevention is the only option — treat CNV within 1–2 weeks of onset.

**2. Geographic atrophy (dry AMD)**
- **Presentation:** Slowly expanding blind spots. Patients may not notice until the fovea is involved — then they lose reading vision.
- **Key signs:** Sharply demarcated depigmented patches at the macula, visible underlying choroidal vessels. OCT shows RPE loss with increased choroidal signal.
- **What to do:** No proven therapy to reverse atrophy. AREDS2 slows progression but does not restore vision. Offer low vision aids.

**3. Bilateral severe vision loss (both eyes advanced AMD)**
- **Presentation:** The patient cannot read, recognise faces, or walk safely. They are functionally blind despite normal peripheral vision (which is preserved).
- **Key signs:** Visual acuity 6/60 or worse in both eyes, intact full visual fields on confrontation.
- **What to do:** Refer to low vision clinic, social services, and certified orientation and mobility specialist. Consider cataract surgery if cataract contributes — but explain that it will not improve AMD-related scotoma.

**Late-presentation pattern (the “missed wet AMD” stem):**
A 70-year-old man presents with “a black spot in the middle of my left eye for 3 months.” He thought it was a floater. Fundoscopy shows a pale, slightly elevated scar at the macula. OCT shows a disciform scar with no fluid. **This is late wet AMD — no treatment benefit.** The stem tests your recognition that anti-VEGF is useless for fibrosed CNV. The question then asks: “What is the most appropriate next step?” **Answer:** Low vision assessment and smoking cessation counseling.

---
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**

- **AREDS** — *A*ge-Related *E*ye *D*isease *S*tudy — also stands for the supplement formula (Vitamin *A*? No — actually no vitamin A in AREDS2. Use instead: **L**utein, **Z**inc, **C**, **E** — but not A.)
- **WET AMD** — *W*avy lines, *E*levation of retina, *T*reatment with anti-VEGF, *M*etamorphopsia, *D*rusen present.
- **DRY AMD** — *D*rusen, *R*etinal pigment epithelium atrophy, *Y*ellow spots (drusen), *A*mblyopia-like scotoma, *M*onitoring with Amsler grid.

**Clinical pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**

1. **Amsler grid is your best cheap test** — every patient with drusen should get one. If the lines suddenly become wavy or missing → call ophthalmology that day.
2. **Drusen are not benign** — even small drusen in an older smoker indicate a 4-fold increased risk of developing advanced AMD within 10 years.
3. **The difference between CSC and wet AMD** — CSC occurs in young men, has no drusen, and the subretinal fluid is clear (on OCT, no hemorrhage). FA shows a single pinpoint leak, not a CNV network. Do not treat CSC with anti-VEGF (it usually resolves on its own).
4. **Anti-VEGF injections are not cataract surgery** — they do not improve vision in dry AMD. Only treat wet AMD.
5. **If a patient on anticoagulation develops wet AMD**, the hemorrhage may be larger, but do not stop anticoagulation without cardiology input. The eye can still be treated.
6. **Bilateral wet AMD is rare at first presentation** — if both eyes have CNV acutely, suspect a systemic cause (e.g., punctate inner choroidopathy, angioid streaks, or myopic CNV). Check for pseudoxanthoma elasticum (angioid streaks) or high myopia (>-6 D).
7. **Smoking cessation is the single most powerful intervention** — it reduces risk of progression by 50% compared to continued smoking.

**Common mistakes to avoid:**
- Telling a patient with dry AMD that “nothing can be done” — AREDS2 and smoking cessation reduce progression; low vision aids help function.
- Treating a central serous chorioretinopathy patient with anti-VEGF because you saw subretinal fluid — wait for OCT signs of CNV (hyperreflective membrane, hemorrhage) or FA finding.
- Missing wet AMD because you only checked pin-hole visual acuity — always ask about distortion and test Amsler grid.
- Prescribing AREDS2 for patients with only early AMD (small drusen, no GA or wet) — no proven benefit, only cost and potential side effects.

---
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
| Situation | What to do | When to escalate |
|-----------|------------|------------------|
| Patient calls with sudden vision loss or distortion in one eye (suspected new wet AMD) | Urgent same-day ophthalmology appointment (via eye casualty or acute referral line) | If symptoms <2 weeks, still time to treat; after 2 weeks, treat may still help but outcomes worse |
| Patient is already on anti-VEGF and develops eye pain, redness, photophobia, and vision drop 2–5 days after injection | Suspect **endophthalmitis** — medical emergency. Immediate ophthalmology review. Do not wait for cultures. | **Call ophthalmologist on-call immediately** — they will perform vitreous tap and inject intravitreal antibiotics (vancomycin + ceftazidime) |
| Patient with known dry AMD experiences sudden worsening (metamorphopsia) | Amsler grid shows new distortion → urgent OCT. Can be done in eye clinic same day. | Escalate to ophthalmology registrar if wet AMD suspected |
| Patient with bilateral advanced AMD and NEWS2 >5 from another cause (e.g., pneumonia) | Standard sepsis care. Remember that the patient may not see the call bell or medication labels. | Inform nursing staff of visual impairment; ensure safe bed environment (no obstacles) |
| Post-injection patient has raised IOP (>30 mmHg) with pain, cloudy cornea | Suspect acute glaucoma crisis (lens-induced or vitreous shift). Requires topical IOP-lowering (acetazolamide 500 mg IV stat, then pilocarpine) | Call ophthalmology urgently; if IOP >50 mmHg and no response within 30 min, consider anterior chamber paracentesis |
| Any patient on anti-VEGF with sudden loss of light perception in injected eye | Suspect retinal artery occlusion or massive subretinal hemorrhage. | **Call crash team?** No — call ophthalmology on-call immediately for emergency assessment. |

**Specific thresholds for escalation:**
- **NEWS2:** Not retina-specific. Use standard criteria. However, note that visual impairment may make the patient appear less aware of surroundings — don’t confuse this with confusion.
- **Lactate:** Not relevant to AMD directly, but if a patient becomes septic from an injection (endophthalmitis can lead to sepsis), lactate >2 may indicate systemic infection.
- **GCS:** If the patient cannot see, assessing GCS requires careful verbal and tactile stimuli. Eye opening to speech may be misinterpreted if the patient cannot see your hand — say “open your eyes” first.
- **Ophthalmic specific:** Loss of light perception = need for immediate ophthalmology review, even if other vitals are normal.

rawMarkdown:
## Age-Related Macular Degeneration (AMD)

**One-liner:** Progressive degeneration of the macula causing irreversible central vision loss; leading cause of blindness in over-50s; requires early detection to preserve reading and driving ability.

---

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

A 78-year-old woman presents with a two-month history of “a blurry patch in the middle of my vision” when reading. She also notices that straight door frames appear wavy. She has a 40-pack-year smoking history and her mother went blind in old age. On examination, her visual acuity is 6/18 in the right eye and 6/9 in the left. Fundoscopy reveals numerous yellow-white spots at the macula in both eyes, with a grey-green elevation and a small subretinal hemorrhage in the right eye.

**Dissecting the clues:**
- **Age >65 and gradual onset** → onset is typically insidious for dry AMD; sudden onset of distortion suggests conversion to wet AMD.
- **“Blurry patch in centre, not sides”** → macula disease spares peripheral fields; patients complain of central scotoma.
- **Metamorphopsia (wavy lines)** → highly specific for choroidal neovascularisation (CNV) — fluid under the neurosensory retina distorts photoreceptors.
- **Smoking history** → single strongest modifiable risk factor; oxidative stress and complement activation.
- **Family history** → genetic variants in CFH and ARMS2 are strong predictors; ask about blindness in parents/siblings.
- **Yellow-white spots (drusen)** → hallmark of early AMD; they are deposits of lipids and protein between the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) and Bruch’s membrane.
- **Grey-green elevation and hemorrhage** → classic signs of CNV; the “green” colour is the pigment epithelial detachment; blood indicates active leak.

**Atypical presentations:**
1. **Asymptomatic drusen** → many patients have drusen found incidentally during a routine eye exam; they may never progress. The trap is assuming they are benign — 15% develop advanced AMD within 5 years.
2. **Rapid vision loss in one eye (over hours to days)** → can mimic a retinal artery occlusion or inflammatory papillitis. Always check for a relative afferent pupillary defect (RAPD) and perform OCT to look for subretinal fluid.
3. **Bilateral symmetric dry AMD with no drusen** → geographic atrophy (dry advanced AMD) appears as sharply demarcated zones of RPE atrophy; the patient may not notice until fovea is involved. Can be mistaken for retinal dystrophies if the classic drusen are absent.

---

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

Age-related macular degeneration is a chronic, progressive degeneration of the macula — the central 5 mm of the retina responsible for high-acuity vision (reading, face recognition, driving). It occurs in two forms: **non-exudative (dry)** , characterised by drusen and geographic atrophy, and **exudative (wet)** , driven by choroidal neovascularisation that leaks fluid and blood under the retina.

**Why it matters:**
- **Frequency:** AMD affects 1 in 7 people over 50 globally; it is the leading cause of irreversible central vision loss in people over 65 in the developed world.
- **Stakes:** Patients lose the ability to read, recognise faces, and safely navigate — the psychological and functional impact rivals that of hip fracture or stroke.
- **Common pitfalls:** Junior doctors often dismiss “floaters and flashes” as benign vitreous detachment without checking central vision or performing Amsler grid testing. They also mistake the distortion of wet AMD for migraine aura or dry eye. Early diagnosis of wet AMD is a true emergency — every day of delay reduces the chance of functional recovery.

---

### 3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind the Findings

The story of AMD begins with the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE), a single layer of cells that nourishes photoreceptors, recycles visual pigments, and pumps fluid out of the subretinal space. With age, oxidative stress from high metabolic demand and light exposure damages RPE cells. In genetically susceptible individuals (CFH gene variant reduces complement factor H activity), the RPE cannot clear debris effectively. This debris accumulates as **drusen** — yellow-white excrescences beneath the RPE.

**Why drusen appear:** The RPE sheds lysosomal material into Bruch’s membrane; when clearance fails, lipid and protein aggregates form drusen. On fundoscopy they look like yellow “spots”; they are the earliest clinical sign.

**Why vision blurs in dry AMD:** As drusen enlarge and confluent drusen develop, the RPE becomes dysfunctional. Photoreceptors begin to die, especially in the fovea. Eventually, the RPE atrophies in large confluent patches — **geographic atrophy**. The photoreceptors over these patches are gone, producing a well-demarcated scotoma. The patient notices blurring but often compensates with the other eye until the fovea is affected.

**Why vision distorts in wet AMD:** Chronic inflammation and RPE stress upregulate vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF). VEGF diffuses from the RPE to the choriocapillaris, promoting growth of abnormal new blood vessels through Bruch’s membrane into the sub-RPE or subretinal space. These **choroidal neovascular membranes (CNV)** are fragile and leak fluid (serous detachment) and blood (subretinal hemorrhage). The fluid separates photoreceptors from the RPE, causing them to become edematous and misaligned — this produces **metamorphopsia** (wavy lines). The grey-green elevation seen on fundoscopy is the pigment epithelial detachment caused by fluid accumulating under the RPE.

**Connecting to the pattern recognition cues:**
- Drusen → early AMD on exam.
- Metamorphopsia + sudden onset → active CNV → needs urgent anti-VEGF.
- Subretinal hemorrhage on fundoscopy → confirms wet AMD.
- Symmetric slow central vision loss over months to years → dry AMD with geographic atrophy.

---

### 4. Causes / Triggers (Organised in Bins)

| Category | Cause / Trigger | Stem clue that points to it |
|----------|----------------|----------------------------|
| **Idiopathic** | Age >50 | “No other cause found in an elderly patient” |
| **Genetic** | CFH Y402H polymorphism, ARMS2 A69S variant | “Family history of blindness from AMD in a first-degree relative” |
| **Environmental** | Cigarette smoking (10+ pack-years) | “Patient smokes 20 per day” — strongest modifiable risk |
| **Dietary** | Low dietary antioxidants (zinc, lutein, vitamins C/E) | “Poor diet, BMI >30” |
| **Vascular** | Hypertension, cardiovascular disease | “Hypertensive, on statin” |
| **Light exposure** | Chronic sunlight (blue light) | “Worked outdoors for decades without sunglasses” |
| **Drug-related** | Hydroxychloroquine (rare cause of bull’s-eye maculopathy — mimic) | “Long-term hydroxychloroquine for RA” — different pathology |

**Most common cause:** Age alone is the dominant factor. Almost all cases are idiopathic, with genetic predisposition and smoking as the strongest accelerators.

---

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria:** The **AREDS classification** (Age-Related Eye Disease Study) grades AMD into categories:
- Category 1: No drusen or few small drusen (<63 µm) — essentially normal aging.
- Category 2: Multiple small drusen or a few medium drusen (63–124 µm) — early AMD.
- Category 3: Many medium drusen or at least one large drusen (≥125 µm) OR geographic atrophy not involving fovea — intermediate AMD.
- Category 4: Geographic atrophy involving fovea OR CNV — advanced AMD.

**Key investigations:**

| Test | What it shows | Why you order it | How to interpret |
|------|---------------|------------------|------------------|
| **Fundoscopy** | Drusen, RPE changes, subretinal fluid/hemorrhage | First-line; office-based | Presence of drusen = AMD; hemorrhage or grey-green elevation = wet AMD |
| **Optical coherence tomography (OCT)** | Cross-sectional retinal layers; shows drusen, intraretinal fluid, subretinal fluid, PED | Gold standard for wet AMD detection and monitoring | Fluid under the retina (subretinal) or within retina (intraretinal) = active CNV; hyperreflective drusen = dry AMD |
| **Fluorescein angiography (FA)** | Leakage of dye from CNV in wet AMD | Confirms active neovascularisation and classifies type of CNV (type 1 sub-RPE, type 2 subretinal) | Well-defined “lacy” hyperfluorescence with late leakage = classic CNV; ill-defined = occult CNV |
| **Amsler grid** | Patient self-monitoring for distortion or scotoma | Home screening for conversion to wet AMD | New distortion or missing squares = possible CNV → urgent review |
| **Autofluorescence** | Geographic atrophy margins | Quantifies progression of dry AMD | Dark areas with hyperautofluorescent borders = active atrophy expansion |

**Single finding that confirms wet AMD:** Choroidal neovascularisation seen on OCT (subretinal hyperreflective material with fluid) or FA (leaking CNV in the late phase).

**Single finding that rules out wet AMD:** Dry OCT without any fluid, combined with no leakage on FA (if performed).

---

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (first hour) — Suspected wet AMD:**
- **Do not** start any drops or steroids. Do **not** apply pressure to the eye.
- **Immediate referral to ophthalmology** (same-day urgent): Wet AMD is a medical emergency. Vision can be lost permanently within hours to days.
- **No investigations on the ward** beyond checking visual acuity (Snellen chart), pupil reactivity, and performing a basic fundoscopy (if trained). The only definitive test is OCT/FA in the eye department.
- **Why:** Anti-VEGF therapy is most effective when given within 1–2 weeks of symptom onset. Delay reduces final visual acuity gains.

**Ward (24–72 hours) — After ophthalmology assessment:**
- **Anti-VEGF injection** (intravitreal): Ranibizumab 0.5 mg in 0.05 mL, or aflibercept 2 mg in 0.05 mL, or bevacizumab (off-label) 1.25 mg in 0.05 mL. Administered in a dedicated eye treatment room under topical anaesthesia.
- **Post-injection monitoring:** Check intraocular pressure if the patient has pain (transient rise is common; can give topical beta-blocker). Instruct patient regarding warning signs of endophthalmitis (pain, redness, decreased vision) — return if these occur within 1 week.
- **Educate:** Show the patient how to use an Amsler grid daily. Explain that distortion means recurrence — do not wait for an appointment.
- **If dry AMD (no fluid):** No urgent treatment. Start AREDS2 supplements (see below) if intermediate or advanced dry AMD in at least one eye.

**Long-term — Dry AMD:**
- **AREDS2 formula (age-related eye disease study 2):** Vitamin C 500 mg, vitamin E 400 IU, zinc oxide 80 mg, copper 2 mg (as cupric oxide), lutein 10 mg, zeaxanthin 2 mg. One capsule twice daily.
- **Why:** The AREDS/AREDS2 trials showed a 25–30% reduction in progression to advanced AMD in patients with intermediate or advanced dry AMD. It does **not** reverse vision loss.
- **Who gets it:** Anyone with Category 3 or 4 AMD in either eye (i.e., large drusen or geographic atrophy in at least one eye, or wet AMD in the fellow eye).
- **Lifestyle modifications:** Smoking cessation, control of hypertension, weight loss, blue-light protective sunglasses.
- **Low vision aids:** For patients with significant central scotoma, offer magnifiers, telescopes, and electronic reading devices. Refer to low vision clinic.

**What NOT to do:**
- **Do not give AREDS2 supplements to patients with only early AMD (small drusen only)** — no proven benefit and may cause harm (excess zinc can cause copper deficiency anaemia; high-dose vitamin E linked to increased prostate cancer risk).
- **Do not treat dry AMD with anti-VEGF** — no benefit and risks infection/retinal detachment.
- **Do not prescribe oral carbonic anhydrase inhibitors for central serous chorioretinopathy if you suspect wet AMD** — that is a different disease.
- **Do not tell a patient with wet AMD “it’s just cataracts”** — cortical cataracts cause glare, not metamorphopsia.

---

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | AMD (wet) | Central Serous Chorioretinopathy (CSC) | Diabetic Macular Edema (DME) | Epiretinal Membrane (ERM) |
|---------|-----------|----------------------------------------|------------------------------|---------------------------|
| **Pain** | None | None | None | None |
| **Laterality** | Usually unilateral (wet) or bilateral (dry) | Typically unilateral | Often bilateral | Usually unilateral |
| **Age** | >65 | <50, male predominance | Any age with diabetes | >50 |
| **Onset** | Subacute (days to weeks) for wet; gradual for dry | Acute (days) | Chronic, fluctuates | Very gradual (months to years) |
| **Metamorphopsia** | Yes (fluid distorts retina) | Yes (serous detachment distorts) | Yes (cystoid edema distorts) | Yes (membrane contracts retina) |
| **Fundoscopy** | Drusen + subretinal fluid/hemorrhage | Well-circumscribed serous detachment (clear fluid, no drusen) | Hard exudates, microaneurysms, dot-blot hemorrhages, retinal thickening | Cellophane-like shimmer on macular surface, vessels tortuous |
| **OCT** | Subretinal fluid, PED, CNV | Serous PED or subretinal fluid without CNV; absent drusen | Intraretinal cysts, increased central thickness, exudates | Epiretinal hyperreflective membrane, macular pseudohole |
| **FA** | Leaking CNV (classic or occult) | Ink-blot leak or smokestack from RPE | Microaneurysms + diffuse leakage | Staining of membrane, no leak |
| **Key differentiator** | Drusen + CNV on OCT | No drusen, young, stressed, unilateral serous detachment | History of diabetes + hard exudates | Pearly membrane on OCT without fluid |

---

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

**1. Irreversible central vision loss (wet AMD)**
- **Presentation:** Sudden drop in visual acuity to counting fingers or hand motion. The CNV bleeds or forms a fibrovascular scar (disciform scar). The scar is white/yellow on fundoscopy — it replaces the macula permanently.
- **Key signs:** No further distortion (because the retina is fixed by fibrosis), dense central scotoma, RAPD if very severe.
- **What to do:** None can reverse a disciform scar. Prevention is the only option — treat CNV within 1–2 weeks of onset.

**2. Geographic atrophy (dry AMD)**
- **Presentation:** Slowly expanding blind spots. Patients may not notice until the fovea is involved — then they lose reading vision.
- **Key signs:** Sharply demarcated depigmented patches at the macula, visible underlying choroidal vessels. OCT shows RPE loss with increased choroidal signal.
- **What to do:** No proven therapy to reverse atrophy. AREDS2 slows progression but does not restore vision. Offer low vision aids.

**3. Bilateral severe vision loss (both eyes advanced AMD)**
- **Presentation:** The patient cannot read, recognise faces, or walk safely. They are functionally blind despite normal peripheral vision (which is preserved).
- **Key signs:** Visual acuity 6/60 or worse in both eyes, intact full visual fields on confrontation.
- **What to do:** Refer to low vision clinic, social services, and certified orientation and mobility specialist. Consider cataract surgery if cataract contributes — but explain that it will not improve AMD-related scotoma.

**Late-presentation pattern (the “missed wet AMD” stem):**
A 70-year-old man presents with “a black spot in the middle of my left eye for 3 months.” He thought it was a floater. Fundoscopy shows a pale, slightly elevated scar at the macula. OCT shows a disciform scar with no fluid. **This is late wet AMD — no treatment benefit.** The stem tests your recognition that anti-VEGF is useless for fibrosed CNV. The question then asks: “What is the most appropriate next step?” **Answer:** Low vision assessment and smoking cessation counseling.

---

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**

- **AREDS** — *A*ge-Related *E*ye *D*isease *S*tudy — also stands for the supplement formula (Vitamin *A*? No — actually no vitamin A in AREDS2. Use instead: **L**utein, **Z**inc, **C**, **E** — but not A.)
- **WET AMD** — *W*avy lines, *E*levation of retina, *T*reatment with anti-VEGF, *M*etamorphopsia, *D*rusen present.
- **DRY AMD** — *D*rusen, *R*etinal pigment epithelium atrophy, *Y*ellow spots (drusen), *A*mblyopia-like scotoma, *M*onitoring with Amsler grid.

**Clinical pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**

1. **Amsler grid is your best cheap test** — every patient with drusen should get one. If the lines suddenly become wavy or missing → call ophthalmology that day.
2. **Drusen are not benign** — even small drusen in an older smoker indicate a 4-fold increased risk of developing advanced AMD within 10 years.
3. **The difference between CSC and wet AMD** — CSC occurs in young men, has no drusen, and the subretinal fluid is clear (on OCT, no hemorrhage). FA shows a single pinpoint leak, not a CNV network. Do not treat CSC with anti-VEGF (it usually resolves on its own).
4. **Anti-VEGF injections are not cataract surgery** — they do not improve vision in dry AMD. Only treat wet AMD.
5. **If a patient on anticoagulation develops wet AMD**, the hemorrhage may be larger, but do not stop anticoagulation without cardiology input. The eye can still be treated.
6. **Bilateral wet AMD is rare at first presentation** — if both eyes have CNV acutely, suspect a systemic cause (e.g., punctate inner choroidopathy, angioid streaks, or myopic CNV). Check for pseudoxanthoma elasticum (angioid streaks) or high myopia (>-6 D).
7. **Smoking cessation is the single most powerful intervention** — it reduces risk of progression by 50% compared to continued smoking.

**Common mistakes to avoid:**
- Telling a patient with dry AMD that “nothing can be done” — AREDS2 and smoking cessation reduce progression; low vision aids help function.
- Treating a central serous chorioretinopathy patient with anti-VEGF because you saw subretinal fluid — wait for OCT signs of CNV (hyperreflective membrane, hemorrhage) or FA finding.
- Missing wet AMD because you only checked pin-hole visual acuity — always ask about distortion and test Amsler grid.
- Prescribing AREDS2 for patients with only early AMD (small drusen, no GA or wet) — no proven benefit, only cost and potential side effects.

---

### 10. Escalation Criteria

| Situation | What to do | When to escalate |
|-----------|------------|------------------|
| Patient calls with sudden vision loss or distortion in one eye (suspected new wet AMD) | Urgent same-day ophthalmology appointment (via eye casualty or acute referral line) | If symptoms <2 weeks, still time to treat; after 2 weeks, treat may still help but outcomes worse |
| Patient is already on anti-VEGF and develops eye pain, redness, photophobia, and vision drop 2–5 days after injection | Suspect **endophthalmitis** — medical emergency. Immediate ophthalmology review. Do not wait for cultures. | **Call ophthalmologist on-call immediately** — they will perform vitreous tap and inject intravitreal antibiotics (vancomycin + ceftazidime) |
| Patient with known dry AMD experiences sudden worsening (metamorphopsia) | Amsler grid shows new distortion → urgent OCT. Can be done in eye clinic same day. | Escalate to ophthalmology registrar if wet AMD suspected |
| Patient with bilateral advanced AMD and NEWS2 >5 from another cause (e.g., pneumonia) | Standard sepsis care. Remember that the patient may not see the call bell or medication labels. | Inform nursing staff of visual impairment; ensure safe bed environment (no obstacles) |
| Post-injection patient has raised IOP (>30 mmHg) with pain, cloudy cornea | Suspect acute glaucoma crisis (lens-induced or vitreous shift). Requires topical IOP-lowering (acetazolamide 500 mg IV stat, then pilocarpine) | Call ophthalmology urgently; if IOP >50 mmHg and no response within 30 min, consider anterior chamber paracentesis |
| Any patient on anti-VEGF with sudden loss of light perception in injected eye | Suspect retinal artery occlusion or massive subretinal hemorrhage. | **Call crash team?** No — call ophthalmology on-call immediately for emergency assessment. |

**Specific thresholds for escalation:**
- **NEWS2:** Not retina-specific. Use standard criteria. However, note that visual impairment may make the patient appear less aware of surroundings — don’t confuse this with confusion.
- **Lactate:** Not relevant to AMD directly, but if a patient becomes septic from an injection (endophthalmitis can lead to sepsis), lactate >2 may indicate systemic infection.
- **GCS:** If the patient cannot see, assessing GCS requires careful verbal and tactile stimuli. Eye opening to speech may be misinterpreted if the patient cannot see your hand — say “open your eyes” first.
- **Ophthalmic specific:** Loss of light perception = need for immediate ophthalmology review, even if other vitals are normal.
